Reflection: In today's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that HAMs are medications that bear a heightened risk of causing significant patient harm when used in error, and that the Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) publishes a widely-used list that groups them both by class (e.g., anticoagulants such as heparin and warfarin, insulins, opioids, neuromuscular blocking agents, and concentrated electrolytes such as potassium chloride) and by specific drug name. I also learned that The Joint Commission requires hospitals to maintain their own facility-specific HAM list tailored to prescribing patterns and patient populations, and to implement targeted risk-reduction strategies at every stage of the medication use process: prescribing, transcribing, dispensing, administering, and monitoring. We also reviewed the hierarchy of safety interventions — from high-leverage strategies like forcing functions (e.g., removing concentrated KCl from floor stock) and constraints, to medium-leverage strategies like standardization and barcoding, down to low-leverage strategies like education and reminders. This learning matters for my growth because it reframes how I think about patient safety. I used to assume that catching an error at the bedside through careful double-checking was the core of safe practice, but this session challenged that assumption by showing me that independent double-checks are a low-to-medium power strategy and can fail under time pressure or cognitive load. Understanding the power hierarchy pushes me to evaluate safety interventions more critically and to advocate for system-level fixes rather than relying on personal vigilance. It also deepens my appreciation for why a pharmacist's role extends beyond verification and into operational design. I will apply this learning during my hospital rotation by first asking my preceptor for the institutional HAM list and reviewing the specific safeguards in place — for example, smart-pump drug libraries for insulin infusions, tall-man lettering for look-alike/sound-alike HAMs, and mandatory independent double-checks for pediatric chemotherapy. When I encounter a HAM order, I will intentionally pause to verify indication, dose, route, and monitoring parameters before verifying it. If I identify a gap — for example, a HAM without an associated hard-stop in the order set — I will document it and bring it to my preceptor to discuss whether a higher-leverage safeguard could be added.

In today's session, I learned that high-alert medications (HAMs) are drugs that have a heightened risk of causing significant patient harm when used in error, and that the Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) publishes a list of these medications that includes classes such as anticoagulants, insulins, opioids, neuromuscular blockers, and concentrated electrolytes. I also learned that hospitals are required to maintain their own facility-specific HAM list and to use a range of error-reduction strategies including forcing functions, standardization, and education. This matters because medication errors can seriously harm patients, and HAMs in particular can lead to severe outcomes. It's important for pharmacists to know about these medications so we can help keep patients safe. In the future, I will try to be more careful with HAMs and follow the hospital's safety procedures when I see them.

Today's class covered an important topic in patient safety. Medications can sometimes cause harm, and it is important for healthcare workers to be careful. This matters because patient safety is one of the most important goals in healthcare, and pharmacists play a key role. I will keep this in mind going forward and try to do my best in practice.

In this week's session, we discussed high-alert medications and how errors can happen at different points along the way. We talked about how hospitals try to reduce these risks using different strategies. This learning matters to me because it changed my understanding of where medication safety actually lives. I used to think about safety as happening at the final step — the pharmacist checking the order or the nurse administering the drug — but this session helped me realize that errors are often introduced much earlier, at the prescribing or transcribing stage, and the later steps are just the last chance to catch them. This is important for my development because it shifts my mental model from "catch errors at the end" to "design each step to make errors less likely in the first place." It also makes me more aware that my own future decisions as a pharmacist — the order sets I use, the clarifications I ask for, how I communicate with nurses — are all part of a shared system. I want to approach HAMs with that systems mindset rather than relying on individual carefulness. In the future, I will try to be mindful of HAMs when I see them at work.

In this week's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that errors involving HAMs can occur at any of the five stages of the medication use process — prescribing, transcribing, dispensing, administering, and monitoring — and that targeted safeguards need to be layered at each stage rather than concentrated at a single point. Specific examples we reviewed included dose-range checks and standardized concentration libraries at prescribing; computerized provider order entry (CPOE) with embedded clinical decision support to eliminate transcription; barcode medication administration (BCMA) and independent double-checks at dispensing and administering; and protocolized laboratory monitoring (e.g., anti-Xa or INR for anticoagulants, glucose checks for insulin drips) during monitoring. We also discussed how many sentinel events involving HAMs like heparin and insulin trace back to upstream issues such as look-alike packaging or ambiguous orders, rather than failures at the bedside. This learning matters for my growth because it reshapes how I conceptualize medication safety. Previously I assumed the pharmacist's main contribution was the verification step, but this session showed me that the most leverage actually comes from designing the upstream system — the order set, the product selection, the storage configuration — so that the dangerous action becomes difficult or impossible. It also challenged my assumption that errors reflect individual inattention; in reality, well-intentioned clinicians can be set up to fail by poorly designed processes. That insight is foundational to how I want to practice. I will apply this in my APPE rotations by asking to review at least one sentinel event or medication-error case involving a HAM at each site and tracing where along the medication use process the failure occurred. When I verify HAM orders, I will not just confirm the dose is appropriate but also note whether upstream safeguards like indication-linked order sets and concentration standardization were used. If I identify a recurring weak point — for example, nurses repeatedly calling to clarify a heparin protocol — I plan to bring it up in a P&T or medication safety committee meeting as a candidate for a system-level fix.

Today we talked about medications that can be dangerous and the different steps of giving medications to patients. Errors can happen at any step. This is just part of the course, so we need to know it. At my internship, I will watch for mistakes with these medications and try to be careful.

Today we talked about high-alert medications and how some of them can look alike or sound alike, which can cause confusion. This is an important topic in pharmacy. I will try to pay attention to drug names at my internship and be careful when I dispense medications that look or sound similar.

In today's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that a significant share of HAM errors stem from look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) confusion and packaging-related failures. ISMP and the FDA maintain confusable-name pair lists — for example, hydroxyzine vs. hydralazine, DOBUTamine vs. DOPamine, and insulin products with similar branding across concentrations (U-100 vs. U-500 regular insulin) — and recommend specific mitigations such as tall-man lettering (e.g., "DOBUTamine"/"DOPamine"), physical separation of storage bins, distinct auxiliary labels, and purchasing from different manufacturers when possible to avoid near-identical vials. We also discussed how unit-dose packaging, barcode scanning at dispensing and administration, and removal of concentrated electrolytes from floor stock have been shown in the literature to meaningfully reduce HAM errors at the system level. This matters for my development because it concretely illustrates that "being careful" is not sufficient protection against errors that are built into the physical environment. I had not previously thought about the vial label, the bin location, or even the typeface as safety interventions, but the session helped me see that these are exactly the high-leverage levers that remove reliance on memory and vigilance. This reframes the pharmacist's role as partly that of a human-factors designer: every choice about storage, labeling, and workflow is a safety decision. It also makes me more humble about my own susceptibility to LASA errors — I now understand that I can be well-trained and still mix up DOBUTamine and DOPamine if the system sets me up to. I will apply this at my hospital rotation by doing a walk-through of the automated dispensing cabinet and pharmacy shelves with my preceptor and identifying any LASA pairs that are stored adjacent to each other, flagging them for separation or additional labeling. I also plan to review our facility's tall-man lettering policy to check that it aligns with the current ISMP list, and to observe a BCMA workflow at administration to understand where an unscanned HAM would still be caught. If I see a near-miss involving a LASA HAM, I will write it up through the institution's event-reporting system so the pattern can be tracked and addressed.

ISMP and the FDA publish lists of confusable name pairs — such as hydralazine vs. hydroxyzine and DOBUTamine vs. DOPamine — and recommend mitigations including tall-man lettering, physical separation of storage, distinct auxiliary labeling, and purchasing from multiple manufacturers so that similar-looking vials do not sit side by side on the shelf. This matters for my development because it illustrates that "being careful" is not sufficient protection against errors that are built into the physical environment. I had not previously thought about a vial label or a storage bin location as safety interventions, but the session made me see that these are actually high-leverage levers because they reduce reliance on memory and vigilance. That reframes the pharmacist's role — we are partly human-factors designers, and every choice about storage, labeling, and workflow is a safety decision. It also makes me more humble about my own susceptibility to LASA errors, because I now understand that I can be well-trained and still mix up two similar names if the system sets me up to. In my future practice, I want to be thoughtful about LASA medications and do my part to reduce these kinds of errors.

In this week's class, I learned how safety strategies differ across specific high-alert medications based on the type of harm each one causes. For insulin, key safeguards include standardized U-100 concentration (with U-500 kept out of floor stock and dispensed in patient-specific doses only), bedside blood-glucose verification before administration, and avoiding the abbreviation "U" on handwritten orders because it is frequently misread as a zero and leads to ten-fold overdoses. For heparin, safeguards include standardized IV concentrations, weight-based dosing protocols with built-in maximums, anti-Xa or aPTT monitoring protocols, and separate storage of unfractionated heparin from low-molecular-weight heparin to prevent product mix-ups. For chemotherapy, safeguards include a two-pharmacist independent verification of the order and the compounded product, patient-specific body-surface-area (BSA) dose checks, verification of cumulative lifetime doses for agents like anthracyclines (to prevent irreversible cardiotoxicity), and administration only by credentialed oncology nurses. This learning matters for my growth because it teaches me that high-alert medication safety is not a single generic checklist — it is a set of drug-specific, failure-mode-specific defenses. Before this session I thought of HAMs as a homogeneous category that required uniform extra care. Now I understand that the "extra care" is shaped by the pharmacology and the most likely error for that specific drug: hypoglycemia from insulin, bleeding from heparin, irreversible cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines. That insight pushes me to learn each HAM's mechanism and failure modes deeply rather than treating them as interchangeable. I will apply this during my hospital IPPE and APPE rotations by building a personal reference for the top five HAMs at each site — what the most common errors are, what safeguards exist at that institution, and where the safeguards have known gaps. When I verify an insulin or heparin order, I will explicitly check the concentration, weight-based dose, and indication against the protocol rather than relying only on whether the order "looks reasonable." For chemotherapy, I will shadow the oncology pharmacist's independent verification process and practice the checks under supervision before performing them myself.

Today's class was about high-alert medications like insulin, heparin, and chemotherapy. These medications can cause harm if something goes wrong. This is important because patient safety depends on being careful with these drugs. Pharmacists play a role in keeping patients safe from medication errors.

Today's session focused on the independent double-check (IDC) as a high-alert medication safeguard, and on the difference between an effective IDC and a performative one. I learned that an independent double-check requires two qualified individuals to independently verify the patient, drug, dose, concentration, route, rate, and infusion programming without one priming the other (e.g., not saying "this is heparin 25,000 units, can you check?" but rather presenting only the order and the prepared product so the second checker must arrive at the verification independently). We reviewed ISMP guidance that IDCs are most appropriate for narrow-therapeutic-index HAMs (insulin, opioid infusions, chemotherapy, neuromuscular blockers) and not as a universal control — overusing IDCs erodes their effectiveness because nurses begin to rubber-stamp checks they perform constantly.

This learning shifted how I think about pharmacy safety controls. I had previously considered "more checks = safer" without recognizing that a poorly designed double-check can give false reassurance and crowd out attention to other safeguards. The distinction between an independent check and a confirmation check was the most useful piece — it explained to me why some hospitals report that their IDC compliance rate is high but their HAM error rate has not improved.

I will apply this on my hospital rotation by (1) observing at least three independent double-check events with my preceptor and noting whether they meet ISMP's independence criteria or are confirmation checks in disguise; (2) asking the medication safety officer how the institution decides which medications trigger an IDC and how that list is reviewed; and (3) practicing the technique of presenting only the order and the product when I am the first checker, so that I am building the habit before I am responsible for the verification independently.

Today we covered independent double-checks for high-alert medications. The idea is that two people independently verify the medication before it is given to the patient. We talked about how the check is most useful for medications like insulin and heparin and that doing it for everything makes it less effective. I think this is useful information. I will pay attention to how double-checks are done at my internship.
Today's class was about double-checking medications. Two people check the medication. This helps prevent errors. I will keep this in mind.

This session covered smart infusion pump technology and dose error reduction systems (DERS) as engineering controls for high-alert medication safety. I learned that smart pumps house a customizable drug library — created and maintained by the pharmacy — that includes hard limits (which the pump will not let the user override) and soft limits (which alert but allow override with documented reason). The library is segmented by clinical care area (adult ICU, NICU, oncology) so that, for example, a heparin concentration that is acceptable in adult cardiac is hard-blocked in pediatric oncology. We also reviewed ISMP-recommended outcome measures: drug library compliance rate, rate of soft-limit overrides, and the proportion of "saved" programming errors caught by hard limits.

What surprised me was learning that the pump library is only as safe as its ongoing maintenance. Pharmacy teams must regularly review override data and near-miss reports to refine concentrations, rates, and limits — otherwise the library drifts out of date and clinicians override it routinely. This reframed smart pump deployment from a one-time technology purchase to an ongoing pharmacy stewardship activity. I had not appreciated how much of medication safety technology depends on the pharmacist's analytical work behind the scenes.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether I can review the most recent smart pump override report for one care unit and identify any high-frequency overrides that suggest a library update is needed; (2) shadowing the medication safety pharmacist if the institution has one to learn how library changes are vetted and rolled out; and (3) committing to look at infusion pump technology not as a black box but as a pharmacy-curated safety asset that I will eventually be responsible for maintaining.

Today we learned about smart infusion pumps and how they help prevent dose errors. Smart pumps have a drug library with limits that warn or block the user from programming wrong doses. This helps prevent harm to patients, especially with high-alert medications. I will pay attention to how smart pumps are used at my hospital rotation.
Smart pumps help with medication safety. They have programs that prevent errors. This is good for patients.

This week's class focused on why pediatric patients are at disproportionately high risk for high-alert medication errors and the engineering, process, and communication safeguards specific to that population. I learned that nearly every dose in pediatrics is weight-based, that small math errors produce 10-fold or 100-fold dosing errors more often than in adults, and that concentration confusion (especially with morphine, fentanyl, and heparin) is a leading driver of pediatric HAM events. We also reviewed safeguards: standardized concentrations across the institution, pre-printed pediatric order sets that pre-calculate doses, mandatory weight verification in kg only (no lbs in the EHR), and dispensing in patient-specific rather than floor-stock form for the highest-risk products.

This learning changed my picture of pediatric pharmacy practice in a couple of ways. I had previously thought of pediatric dosing as primarily a calculation skill. After this class I understand it as a system-design problem in which the pharmacist's role is to remove opportunities for the calculation to be wrong — through standard concentrations, smart pump libraries with pediatric hard limits, and dispensing decisions that put the right dose in the right container. The personal piece for me was realizing that, in a pediatric rotation, my responsibility is not only to verify doses correctly but to advocate for upstream system fixes when I notice a recurring near-miss pattern.

I will apply this by (1) requesting a pediatric rotation week if my program permits, with the explicit goal of observing how pediatric HAM dispensing differs from adult; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through one pediatric near-miss report and the system change (or non-change) it triggered; and (3) building a personal checklist for myself that I will use whenever I verify a pediatric HAM order — confirm weight in kg, confirm concentration matches institutional standard, confirm dose is within published pediatric range, and double-check the math against an independent reference.

This week we learned that pediatric patients are at higher risk for medication errors because most doses are based on weight and small mistakes can become big ones. We talked about how pharmacies use standardized concentrations and pediatric-specific order sets to reduce these errors. This matters because children are vulnerable to medication harm. I will be careful with pediatric dosing during my rotations.
We talked about how children need different doses than adults. Pediatric medications can be tricky. Pharmacists have to be careful.

This session covered anticoagulation management at transitions of care, which the ISMP and Joint Commission identify as one of the highest-risk periods for HAM-related patient harm. I learned that errors cluster at three transitions: (1) hospital admission, where home anticoagulant regimens are not reconciled and patients receive duplicate therapy; (2) intra-hospital transitions (e.g., ICU to floor) where bridging plans are dropped or doses are restarted incorrectly after procedures; and (3) discharge, where DOACs are converted back from heparin without clear instructions and patients leave without filled prescriptions or follow-up. We reviewed evidence-based safeguards: a pharmacist-led anticoagulation reconciliation at each transition, a structured discharge counseling encounter that includes teach-back, and post-discharge follow-up calls within 48–72 hours.

What struck me was the data on how often anticoagulant transitions go wrong even at high-performing institutions, and how often the pharmacist is the only team member who can catch the error. I had previously thought of "medication reconciliation" as a generic checkbox activity. This session reframed it as the highest-leverage anticoagulation safety intervention available, and as an explicit pharmacy responsibility that I should pursue rather than wait to be asked about.

I plan to apply this by (1) asking my preceptor on an inpatient rotation if I can lead the anticoagulation reconciliation for at least two patients per shift, with their oversight; (2) shadowing a discharge counseling encounter for a newly anticoagulated patient and using teach-back myself before the rotation ends; and (3) reviewing the institution's anticoagulation transition protocol and identifying one gap I can write up for my preceptor as a learning exercise.

Today we discussed how patients on anticoagulants can have errors when they move between care settings, like from the hospital to home. Pharmacists need to reconcile their medications carefully and counsel them at discharge so they know what to take. This is important because anticoagulants are high-alert and errors can lead to bleeding or clots. I will pay attention to anticoagulation reconciliation in my rotations.
Anticoagulants are high-alert medications. They can cause harm. Pharmacists have to be careful when patients are switching settings.
